title: Origin Fairness, Politeness, Retries, Backoff, and p-limit · Compiler Case: Origin Buckets
summary: One global concurrency number bursts a single origin, retries synchronize, Retry-After is ignored, and p-limit is treated as a complete scheduler. Predict authorization, URL identity, runtime admission, Promise ownership, resource bounds, accessibility impact, failure, and evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the crawler ts origin buckets increment in publisher-respecting origin scheduler. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-c…

Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove per-origin eligibility, retry classification, Retry-After, bounded jitter, p-limit boundaries, and clock control; every Promise, stream, listener, timer, socket, file, browser context, and report sink needs one cleanup owner; then change one HTML repair, Cheerio mode, selector, unknown value, record schema, link relation, or metadata case and retain rejected evidence. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Maintain per-origin concurrency, minimum spacing, next-eligible time, request and byte budgets, and owner overrides.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Origin Buckets model · Theory Origin Buckets, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: Global concurrency alone guarantees publisher fairness.
